exemplar.title: A Replication of Loftus and Palmerâs Study of Reconstructive Memory in 1974

detailedExemplarData.title: A Replication of Loftus and Palmerâs Study of Reconstructive Memory in 1974

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

versions.error: Durable Object reset because its code was updated.

strengths: Clear and well-stated aim with strong relevance to reconstructive memory theory and Loftus & Palmerâs findings
Accurate description of schema theory and explicit link to the IV of verb intensity
Fully operationalised hypotheses with correctly identified IV and DV
weaknesses: Minor stylistic lapses in writing clarity
Abrupt ending in the summary of Loftus and Palmerâs study suggests a missing linkage sentence
Could deepen the theoretical mechanism discussion to justify the rationale
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction fulfils all top-band requirements: the aim is explicit and its relevance is explained, the theory/model is correctly described and linked, and the hypotheses are fully operationalised. Minor stylistic flaws and a truncated summary of the original study prevent the award of the maximum 6, so a score of 5 reflects a very strong but not flawless performance.

strengths: Independent-measures design is not only described but its reduction of order effects and demand characteristics is explained
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are clearly stated with a note on generalisability
Controlled variables and key materials are listed with justifications, and participant flow is detailed
weaknesses: Justification of sample size and deeper volunteer-bias discussion are lacking
Materials (e.g., pens, computer) are not explicitly justified
Does not discuss potential drawbacks of the chosen design
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section consistently meets the 3â4 band: design choices, sampling, and control are all explained. However, superficial coverage of sample-size rationale, materials justification, and design trade-offs keeps this at the middle of the band, meriting a score of 3 out of 4.

strengths: Appropriate use of median, range, SD and a Mann-Whitney U test with transparent calculations
Graph is neatly labelled, clearly addresses the hypothesis, and uses correct graphing conventions
Findings are correctly linked to hypothesis with explicit inference from the statistical test
weaknesses: Typographical errors in the p-value (âp < 0.5â instead of âp < .05â) occur twice
No formal outlier detection method is applied
Graph lacks error bars or confidence intervals, limiting interpretability
Integration of descriptive statistics into the narrative interpretation could be stronger
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis demonstrates appropriate and mostly accurate application of both descriptive and inferential statistics, correct graphical presentation, and clear interpretation. However, recurring typos in significance levels, lack of error bars, and incomplete statistical discussion prevent full marks, so it is placed in the upper middle of the 3â4 band with a score of 4.

strengths: Discussion explicitly connects findings back to reconstructive memory theory
Strengths and limitations are stated and each is related to validity issues
Modifications are concrete and are linked to the identified limitations
weaknesses: Discussion is largely confirmatory and lacks deeper theoretical critique or alternative explanations
Ethical considerations (e.g., showing crash footage) are not addressed
Evaluation of limitations could be more concise and focused on the most impactful issues
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation addresses all required strands with clear links to theory, relevance of strengths/limitations, and justified modifications. However, it remains at a descriptive rather than a critical level in places, and misses discussion of alternative explanations and ethical dimensions. This situates it in the mid 3â4 band, earning 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph clearly labels both axes (word used vs median speed in km/h), includes units, and directly contrasts the two conditions, making the link to the hypothesis explicit.  Layout is neat though not perfect (no error bars), so criteria for âcorrectly presented and addresses the hypothesisâ are met; 5/6 is appropriate.

The student states the statistical outcome (U = 22.5 < 27, p < .05), explicitly rejects the null, and links this to support for the alternative hypothesis.  Interpretation is tied to the observed data and verbsâ intensity.  While adequate, the explanation could integrate descriptive statistics more fully; hence 5 rather than the maximum 6.

Both null and alternative hypotheses are clearly written, the IV (verb: âhitâ vs âbumpedâ) and DV (numerical speed estimate in km/h) are correctly identified and fully operationalised.  Exact wording of the question is provided, and the DV is specified as a quantitative figure, fulfilling every requirement of the top band with no evident weakness; hence the full 6 marks within the band are merited.

Convenience/volunteer sampling is identified, participant characteristics (>16 IB students, non-psychology) are outlined, and generalisability issues are noted, thereby explaining why this method was chosen.  The explanation is adequate but somewhat surface-level (e.g., no discussion of cultural mix or sample size justification), so a middle score of 3 in the 3-4 band is appropriate.

Reconstructive memory and schema theory are summarised accurately, and the student explains how leading questions might distort memory, explicitly linking the chosen IV (verb intensity) to that theory.  Although the theoretical overview is fairly brief, it is factually sound and the connection to the present investigation is explicit, meeting the âdescribed and link explainedâ criterion.  Minor omissions (e.g., deeper mechanism discussion) keep it at 5 rather than 6.

Findings are related back to reconstructive memory theory and compared to Loftus & Palmer, demonstrating more than a superficial mention.  Nevertheless, the discussion is brief and largely confirmatory, lacking deeper theoretical critique or consideration of alternative explanations, so it fits the middle 3-4 band with a score of 4.

The student states a clear aim â to investigate whether leading verbs affect speed estimation â and then unambiguously explains why this is relevant to the broader debate on reconstructive memory.  By linking the aim to both Bartlettâs original ideas and Loftus & Palmerâs findings, the submission goes beyond merely identifying relevance and shows why the replication matters, satisfying the descriptor for the top band. Minor stylistic flaws do not detract from the clarity of the explanation, so a score of 5 within the 5-6 band is appropriate.

Multiple strengths and limitations are not only listed but explained in relation to internal validity, participant variability, ecological validity, etc., and each is clearly connected to how it might influence the DV.  The coverage is solid though not exhaustive (e.g., ethical issues lightly touched), so 5 in the top band is appropriate.

Key controlled variables (single viewing, identical setting, screening for crash experience) are listed and each is accompanied by a brief justification, showing understanding of why they matter.  Materials are identified, but the rationale for some items (e.g., why the original 1974 clip is preferable) is only implicit.  This fulfills the âexplainedâ standard, albeit not comprehensively, warranting 3 marks.

For every stated limitation the student proposes a concrete modification (matched-pairs on driving experience; selecting drivers; written questions) and justifies how each would mitigate the specific flaw.  The linkage is explicit and coherent, matching the top-band descriptor.  Slight brevity keeps it at 5 rather than 6.

The student not only describes using an independent-measures design but also explains why this choice reduces order effects and demand characteristics.  The explanation demonstrates understanding of how the design aligns with the aim, satisfying the âexplainedâ descriptor.  Because the rationale is concise and does not address potential design trade-offs in depth, a 4 (upper end of the 3-4 band) is justified.

The IA reports medians, ranges, standard deviations and an appropriate non-parametric Mann-Whitney U test, with correct critical comparison and conclusion.  A minor typo (p < 0.5) appears once but the later text and appendix show the correct p < 0.05; calculations are transparent in Appendix V.  Given the overall accuracy and appropriateness, but presence of the small typo, a 5 (not 6) in the top band is awarded.

annotations.comment: The summary of Loftus and Palmerâs original study ends abruptly, indicating a missing concluding sentence. Complete this thought to clearly link their findings to the rationale for the current investigation.

The student provides a clear and coherent summary of Bartlettâs reconstructive memory and schema theory, accurately explaining levelling and sharpening processes. This foundation shows solid understanding of key concepts in memory research.
Both null and alternative hypotheses are stated with the IV and DV correctly identified and operationalised, demonstrating rigorous experimental planning.
Provide a brief justification for why âhitâ is considered higher intensity than âbumpedâ (e.g., lexical norms or pilot ratings) to strengthen the IV operationalisation.
The rationale for using an independent-measures design is well explained, linking reduction of order effects and demand characteristics directly to the investigationâs validity.
Consider discussing potential drawbacks of an independent-measures design (e.g., increased participant variability) to deepen the exploration and rationale for design trade-offs.
The sampling technique is described and a limitation on generalisability is noted, but the student should justify sample size selection and discuss potential volunteer bias in more depth.
Controlled variables are listed with clear operational definitions and justifications, demonstrating thoughtful control of potential confounds.
The materials list includes pens and a computer but lacks justification for these choices; explain why these materials were necessary and how they support experimental control.
The procedure is described in detail, outlining individual participant flow and measures to prevent communication, which strengthens internal validity.
Ethical screening for prior car-crash experience is commendable, demonstrating care for participant welfare and reducing undue stress.
The descriptive analysis correctly uses median, range, and standard deviation, providing a robust summary of central tendency and variability.
The claim that no outliers are present based solely on standard deviation is insufficient; apply a formal outlier detection method (e.g., IQR rule) to support this statement.
Figure 1 is neatly labelled and directly addresses the hypothesis by contrasting verb conditions with median speed estimates.
Include error bars or confidence intervals on the bar graph to visually convey variability and enhance the interpretability of the data.
There is a typo in the inferential write-up: "p < 0.5" should read "p < .05" to accurately reflect the significance level.
The interpretation explicitly connects statistical outcomes back to reconstructive memory theory, reinforcing the investigationâs theoretical grounding.
The discussion would benefit from addressing alternative explanations (e.g., response bias or guessing), which could deepen critical evaluation.
Strengths and limitations are clearly stated with relevance to validity and participant variability, showing strong evaluative skills.
Consider discussing ethical implications of showing car-crash footage to susceptible individuals, which would enrich the limitations section.
The sample evaluation acknowledges driving experience but could be more concise and prioritize the most impactful limitation on speed estimation.
The procedure critique on verbal questioning is insightful; suggest piloting both verbal and written formats to empirically assess potential confounds.
Consider referencing psychometric guidelines on verbal versus written stimuli to bolster modifications proposed for question delivery.
Standardizing question delivery by using printed sheets is a strong procedural modification to eliminate experimenter influence on verb wording perception.
The conclusion repeats an incorrect threshold âp < 0.5.â Correct this to âp < .05â to accurately reflect statistical significance.
